perturbation.original_text: If the Sponsor fails to pay ANTHEMIC within the designated time period, then interest will begin to accrue immediately on the past due amount at the rate of the lesser of the maximum amount allowed by law or 10% annually.
perturbation.changed_text: If the Sponsor fails to pay ANTHEMIC within the designated time period, then interest will begin to accrue immediately on the past due amount at a flat rate of 25% annually.
perturbation.explanation: Usury laws in many states, including California (where the contract is governed), limit the amount of interest that can be charged on a debt. Charging 25% interest could be considered usurious and therefore illegal.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 restricts interest rates for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. While this agreement is for sponsorship, the high interest rate could be challenged under usury laws. The modified text prescribing a flat rate of 25% annually violates this law if it exceeds the maximum rate allowed for loans.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the interest rate from "the lesser of the maximum amount allowed by law or 10% annually" to a flat rate of "25% annually." The original text ensures compliance with usury laws by capping the interest rate at the legal maximum or 10%, whichever is lower. The changed text removes this safeguard and sets a flat rate of 25%, which could violate usury laws if the legal maximum is less than 25%. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Constitution Article XV Section 1, states that the rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money shall be 7 percent per annum, but it shall be competent for the parties to contract in writing for a rate of interest not exceeding 10 percent per annum for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, and a higher rate under certain conditions for other uses. Charging 25% exceeds these limits, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Addresses for notice may be changed from time to time by written notice to the other party. Any communication or payment given by mail will be effective upon the earlier of (a) five business days following deposit in a post office or other official depository under the care and custody of the United States Postal Service; or (b) actual receipt, as indicated by the return receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Addresses for notice may be changed from time to time by written notice to the other party. Any communication or payment given by mail will be effective immediately upon deposit in a post office or other official depository under the care and custody of the United States Postal Service, regardless of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement of actual receipt for notices and payments sent by mail, making them effective immediately upon deposit. This contradicts the legal principle that notice is only effective when it is reasonably likely to have been received, potentially violating due process rights, especially in legal contexts.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 generally adds additional time for responses when service is made by mail. The original text's 'five business days' aligns better with legal norms, while the modified text's 'effective immediately' disregards the allowance for mailing time, potentially prejudicing the receiving party's ability to respond appropriately within legally prescribed timeframes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by making mail notice effective immediately upon deposit, directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013. CCP 1013(a) states that while service is complete at the time of deposit, any period of notice or right to respond is extended by five calendar days (if within California). The changed text eliminates this extension, creating a clear contradiction with the legally mandated timeframe for response. This contradiction falls under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction, because the time-sensitive obligation violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of California without regard to conflicts of law. The proper exclusive venue for resolution of any dispute related to this Agreement is only in Los Angeles, California, and both parties consent to jurisdiction and venue in Los Angeles, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is to be governed and construed according to the laws of the State of California without regard to conflicts of law. The proper exclusive venue for resolution of any dispute related to this Agreement is only in Guam, and both parties consent to jurisdiction and venue in Guam.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the venue to Guam, while maintaining California law, creates potential difficulties in enforcing the agreement, particularly for VNUE, INC, which is based in Seattle, Washington, and ANTHEMIC, LLC, which is based in Los Angeles, California. This could be seen as an unreasonable forum selection clause, making enforcement more difficult and potentially violating principles of fairness and convenience.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S.C. § 1404 doesn't outright prohibit selecting Guam as a venue, it allows a court to transfer a case to another district or division where it might have been brought for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. Selecting Guam, far from both parties' primary locations, could be challenged under this statute as inconvenient and unjust, especially if key witnesses and evidence are located in California or Washington.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the venue for dispute resolution to Guam while maintaining that California law governs the agreement. This creates a potential contradiction because 28 U.S.C. § 1404 allows a court to transfer a case to another district for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue, '(a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented.' Selecting Guam, far from both parties' locations (Washington and California), could be challenged as inconvenient and unjust, especially if key witnesses and evidence are located in California or Washington. While the parties consent initially to the venue, such a distant venue could be viewed as an unreasonable burden on the parties, leading to a potential challenge under 28 U.S.C. § 1404.
